Document [23] (Title: Ciliary Dysfunction -- Evaluation) Additionally, false positives can occur in all the above testing methods due to secondary loss of cilia function. This can occur, for example, if the sample was taken during an acute viral illness that caused transient loss of function. Cell culture can be used to minimize this impact. [25] Genetic testing should also be considered, as 33 genes have been associated with primary ciliary dyskinesia. However, not all of them are included in the commercially available primary ciliary dyskinesia genetic testing panel. As genetic panels continue to develop and advance, genetic testing continues to be a valued test for diagnosis and research. Currently, however, there remain limitations as known PCD patients can have normal genetic testing. [16] In the United States, a combination of ciliary biopsy to demonstrate ciliary ultrastructural abnormalities and genetic testing is used for the diagnosis. In contrast, a combination of genetic testing and high-speed video microscopy is used in Europe.

Document [9] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937) Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with the development of dementia (خرف). In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.
